course.title: Applied Cryptographic Engineering in Go 1.26
course.description: Go developers who can build and test ordinary programs and need to select, implement, verify, migrate, and operate cryptographic systems without inventing cryptography or overstating evidence. This original, research-backed path uses repeated retrieval, cumulative artifacts, changed-case labs, stakeholder projects, and a performance exam.
outcomes: Demonstrate go 1.26.5 and x/crypto v0.54.0; threat modeling; bytes and canonical formats; randomness; secret lifecycle; classical defect labs; aes; modes; aead; hashes; hmac; hkdf; password hashing; modular reasoning; rsa; x25519; ed25519 and ecdsa; hpke; envelope encryption; key management; x.509; tls 1.3; secure protocol state machines; encrypted file formats; ml-kem, ml-dsa, and slh-dsa migration; misuse resistance; vectors, negative tests, fuzzing and interoperability; fips boundaries; operations, incidents, recovery, and release defense through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

Demonstrate runnable deterministic pure-go security decision functions and allowlisted cryptographic api exercises plus explicit go 1.26.5 native compile, os entropy, native timing, vector, fuzz, race, interop, tls, hsm/kms, certificate, load, fault, compliance, recovery, and production transfer gates through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.
Demonstrate five cumulative authentic cryptographic systems and a performance-based production defense examination through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.
sharedRequirements: Every activity retrieves Go correctness, canonical bytes, explicit threat and trust boundaries, purpose-separated keys, bounded attacker-controlled work, uniform safe failures, secret-safe observability, negative tests, and evidence limits before adding one cryptographic boundary.

Browser Go is deterministic and isolated. It models byte identity, formats, nonce and key state, policies, protocol transitions, attacks, migration, and evidence decisions and runs only an allowlisted side-effect-free cryptographic API subset with fixed fixtures. It never generates production keys, proves entropy or constant time, opens networks, contacts TLS, HSM/KMS or certificate systems, reads host secrets, executes commands, or causes external effects.

Passing work requires stable scenario and artifact identity, a named asset and adversary, exact security property, canonical inputs, versioned algorithm and parameter policy, key/nonce ownership, bounded work, observable success and failure, tamper and changed-case evidence, recovery, and explicit native or production transfer limits.

credential.title: Applied Cryptographic Engineering in Go 1.26 Certificate

modules.title: Security Outcomes, Threat Models, and Evidence
modules.description: A medical records product says data is encrypted but cannot name the protected asset, attacker capability, required property, trust boundary, or evidence that would falsify the claim. Learners cumulatively produce cryptographic outcome and evidence map through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Define confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, freshness, forward secrecy, availability, privacy, and accountability as separate observable outcomes.
Model assets, actors, capabilities, entry points, trust boundaries, abuse cases, and acceptable residual risk before choosing a primitive.
Separate mathematical primitive claims, API behavior, implementation evidence, deployment controls, compliance status, and stakeholder outcomes.

modules.title: Bytes, Encodings, and Cryptographic Primitive Selection
modules.description: An API team base64-encodes tokens, labels the result encrypted, hashes secrets for transport, and compares text representations without defining canonical bytes. Learners cumulatively produce byte representation and primitive decision table through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Trace strings, UTF-8, bytes, hexadecimal, base64, binary fields, lengths, and canonical encodings without changing their security meaning.
Distinguish encoding, checksums, hashing, MACs, signatures, encryption, key agreement, KEMs, and password hashing by purpose and reversibility.
Choose a vetted high-level construction that supplies the required combined properties instead of composing low-level primitives casually.

modules.title: Cryptographic Randomness, Entropy, Keys, Salts, and Nonces
modules.description: A service seeds math/rand with time, generates keys and reset tokens from it, retries by reusing a nonce, and cannot prove fork or startup entropy behavior. Learners cumulatively produce randomness and uniqueness contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use current Go cryptographic randomness APIs for keys, tokens, salts, nonces, and blinding while handling failure according to the API contract.
Distinguish entropy sources, deterministic random bit generators, unpredictability, uniqueness, bias, and statistical appearance.
Generate algorithm-compatible keys at supported strengths and retain provenance, purpose, owner, and creation evidence.

modules.title: Secret Bytes, Constant-Time Decisions, and Memory Lifecycle
modules.description: A Go service converts secrets to immutable strings, logs parse failures with raw material, compares tags with ordinary equality, and promises memory erasure the runtime cannot prove. Learners cumulatively produce secret custody and comparison protocol through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Trace secret creation, copies, aliases, use, retention, rotation, destruction limits, logs, crashes, backups, and ownership across Go boundaries.
Minimize conversions and copies of secret-bearing strings and byte slices while documenting unavoidable runtime limits.
Use constant-time comparison APIs for fixed-length secret-dependent values and reject length or parse errors without secret-dependent detail.

modules.title: Classical Ciphers and Cryptanalysis as Defect Labs
modules.description: A training tool presents Caesar, substitution, repeating-key XOR, and one-time pads as equivalent encryption choices and accidentally ships the toy implementation. Learners cumulatively produce classical cipher attack and migration dossier through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Implement and break a bounded shift or substitution cipher to connect keyspace, language redundancy, frequency evidence, and attacker cost.
Demonstrate how repeated XOR keystream use reveals relationships between plaintexts without presenting XOR alone as production encryption.
Explain one-time-pad key length, uniformity, secrecy, single use, distribution, and destruction requirements and their operational cost.

modules.title: AES, Block Ciphers, and Primitive Boundaries
modules.description: A developer calls AES.Encrypt directly on repeated application records, invents padding, and assumes a strong block cipher alone protects a message. Learners cumulatively produce AES primitive boundary and known-answer harness through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Apply AES key-size and fixed block-size contracts without confusing key, block, message, and security strength.
Explain block-cipher keyed permutation behavior and why messages require an approved mode or higher-level construction.
Validate key and block lengths before using low-level AES APIs and convert panics or errors into bounded caller behavior.

modules.title: Modes, Padding, Malleability, and Legacy Decryption
modules.description: A database encrypts records with ECB, a migration endpoint exposes CBC padding failures, and a CTR counter can repeat after restoring a snapshot. Learners cumulatively produce mode failure laboratory and migration adapter through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Demonstrate deterministic block-pattern leakage and prohibit ECB for application messages.
Trace CBC chaining, IV requirements, padding validation, malleability, and oracle behavior as a legacy risk.
Trace CTR keystream generation and prove that key-counter reuse destroys confidentiality and enables malleation.

modules.title: Authenticated Encryption with AES-GCM
modules.description: A multi-tenant service reuses GCM nonces after restart, omits tenant and version context from additional data, and releases plaintext before authentication succeeds. Learners cumulatively produce versioned AES-GCM record envelope through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use AEAD Seal and Open as atomic confidentiality-and-integrity operations and release plaintext only after successful authentication.
Design a crash-, concurrency-, rollback-, and replication-safe GCM nonce strategy within the construction limits.
Bind protocol version, tenant, object identity, algorithm suite, direction, and other non-secret context as canonical associated data.

modules.title: ChaCha20-Poly1305 and Algorithm Choice
modules.description: A mobile product chooses an AEAD from benchmark folklore, mixes XChaCha and IETF nonce formats, and assumes changing algorithms repairs nonce reuse. Learners cumulatively produce portable AEAD selection and compatibility matrix through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use ChaCha20-Poly1305 through its AEAD contract with correct key, nonce, tag, additional-data, and failure handling.
Distinguish standard and XChaCha nonce sizes, interoperability, random-nonce design, and protocol compatibility.
Choose AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305 from platform acceleration, protocol requirements, misuse risk, compliance, interoperability, and measured performance.

modules.title: Cryptographic Hashes, SHA-2, SHA-3, and Commitment Boundaries
modules.description: A download service calls SHA-256 encryption, truncates digests without analysis, uses MD5 for adversarial integrity, and hashes ambiguous JSON. Learners cumulatively produce hash-purpose and digest verification contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Distinguish preimage, second-preimage, collision, length, domain, and security-strength properties required by a use case.
Select supported SHA-2 or SHA-3 functions and output lengths from protocol, interoperability, performance, compliance, and security needs.
Hash bounded streams incrementally, propagate read errors, freeze canonical byte identity, and compare expected digests safely.

modules.title: Message Authentication Codes and HMAC
modules.description: A webhook signs concatenated fields with raw SHA-256, reuses an encryption key, compares hex strings, and accepts the same request repeatedly. Learners cumulatively produce replay-resistant HMAC request authenticator through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use a MAC to provide symmetric origin authentication and integrity while stating its lack of public verifiability and non-repudiation.
Compute and verify HMAC with a supported hash, sufficient independent key, canonical message, and constant-time tag comparison.
Derive or provision separate purpose- and direction-specific MAC keys and bind the use context.

modules.title: Key Derivation, HKDF, and Key Separation
modules.description: A protocol slices one shared secret into keys, uses user passwords as HKDF input, omits transcript context, and changes output length without versioning. Learners cumulatively produce labeled key schedule and derivation tree through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Distinguish extraction, expansion, password stretching, entropy concentration limits, and deterministic key schedules.
Apply HKDF extract and expand with supported hashes, salt, labeled info, bounded output, and one-way key schedule ownership.
Derive independent role, direction, epoch, algorithm, and purpose keys from a versioned hierarchy without key cycles.

modules.title: Password Hashing, Verification, and Upgrade
modules.description: An account service stores unsalted SHA-256 passwords, benchmarks only the happy path, leaks user existence through timing, and cannot upgrade work factors. Learners cumulatively produce versioned password credential verifier through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Store password verifiers with a slow password-hashing construction rather than plaintext, reversible encryption, or fast general hashes.
Select and encode Argon2id memory, time, parallelism, salt, and output parameters from current guidance and measured service budgets.
Choose scrypt or PBKDF2 only from explicit availability and compliance constraints with current calibrated parameters.

modules.title: Modular Arithmetic and Public-Key Reasoning
modules.description: An implementation team manipulates big integers without normalizing signs, confuses modular inverse with division, and mistakes a worked toy example for secure key generation. Learners cumulatively produce bounded modular arithmetic reasoning notebook through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Compute and explain modular equivalence, reduction, addition, multiplication, exponentiation, and negative representatives.
Use Euclid and extended Euclid to determine coprimality and modular inverses and identify non-invertible cases.
Trace square-and-multiply cost and state why secret exponent code must use vetted constant-time implementations.

modules.title: RSA Encryption, Signatures, and Safe Migration
modules.description: A service encrypts large payloads directly with textbook RSA, verifies PKCS#1 v1.5 signatures by default, and accepts any PEM key without purpose or size validation. Learners cumulatively produce RSA-OAEP and RSA-PSS boundary adapter through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Generate or parse supported RSA keys with minimum strength, valid public exponent, purpose, provenance, and bounded encodings.
Use RSA-OAEP only for bounded key material with matching hash and label context and uniform decryption failure.
Hash exact canonical bytes and use RSA-PSS with explicit hash and salt policy for new RSA signatures.

modules.title: ECDH, X25519, and Authenticated Key Agreement
modules.description: A device protocol treats ECDH output as an encryption key, never authenticates peer keys, accepts malformed encodings, and reuses ephemeral keys indefinitely. Learners cumulatively produce authenticated X25519 key-agreement transcript through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use supported ECDH curves through Go interfaces with private/public key separation, strict parsing, and shared-secret error handling.
Select X25519 or approved NIST curves from protocol, hardware, compliance, interoperability, and implementation constraints.
Feed the ECDH result and full context into a labeled KDF rather than using raw shared bytes directly.

modules.title: Digital Signatures with Ed25519 and ECDSA
modules.description: A package signs human-readable JSON, accepts keys from the message being verified, ignores signature context, and reports that signatures prove truth and non-repudiation. Learners cumulatively produce versioned signed artifact verifier through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: State exactly what a valid signature proves about bytes, key control, and context—and what it does not prove about truth, time, authorization, or intent.
Generate, parse, sign, and verify Ed25519 keys and signatures with exact message/context rules and trusted key selection.
Use ECDSA through current Go APIs with approved curves, supported hashes, canonical encodings, and no custom nonce generation.

modules.title: HPKE, Hybrid Encryption, and Envelope Systems
modules.description: A sharing service encrypts one payload separately for every recipient, stores long-lived data keys in metadata, and invents a hybrid construction without binding recipient or suite context. Learners cumulatively produce multi-recipient envelope encryption format through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Combine public-key key establishment with symmetric AEAD for bounded scalable payload encryption and explicit recipient identity.
Use Go HPKE suites and modes with exact KEM, KDF, AEAD, info, associated data, key encoding, and one-shot or context lifecycle.
Generate per-object data keys, wrap them under recipient or key-encryption keys, authenticate metadata, and separate data from key custody.

modules.title: Key Management, Rotation, HSMs, KMS, and Compromise
modules.description: A platform stores a master key in source control, rotates aliases without proving old reads, grants every service decrypt authority, and has no compromise recovery drill. Learners cumulatively produce key lifecycle, custody, and rotation runbook through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Define generation, registration, activation, distribution, use, suspension, rotation, archival, recovery, revocation, destruction, and evidence ownership.
Separate immutable key version, stable logical key ID, mutable alias, algorithm, purpose, state, and cryptoperiod.
Grant narrow sign, verify, encrypt, decrypt, wrap, unwrap, derive, rotate, export, and administer capabilities across separate identities.

modules.title: X.509, PKI, Trust Stores, and Certificate Validation
modules.description: An internal client accepts any certificate signed by a familiar CA, skips hostname checks, ignores key usage and time, and assumes revocation always works online. Learners cumulatively produce certificate path validation and issuance profile through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Parse certificates, names, SANs, key usages, extensions, serials, validity, signatures, and chains without treating parsed data as trusted.
Build and verify a path to an explicit trust anchor with DNS name, time, basic constraints, key usage, policy, and algorithm checks.
Separate system, application, private, tenant, and test roots and prevent accidental trust expansion.

modules.title: TLS 1.3 Configuration and Secure Channels in Go
modules.description: A Go service disables verification to fix staging, pins one leaf certificate forever, exposes secrets in key logs, and claims a successful handshake authenticates application users. Learners cumulatively produce TLS 1.3 client/server security profile through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Trace TLS negotiation, key exchange, certificate or PSK authentication, transcript binding, record protection, resumption, and closure.
Configure current Go TLS defaults, minimum versions, certificates, roots, names, ALPN, timeouts, and safe verification hooks without insecure skip shortcuts.
Model tickets, PSKs, rotation, replay, forward secrecy, and application idempotency before enabling resumption or early data.

modules.title: Secure Protocols, State Machines, Replay, and Downgrade
modules.description: A device pairing protocol encrypts messages but has no explicit roles, transcript, sequence numbers, version negotiation, terminal states, or duplicate policy. Learners cumulatively produce authenticated protocol state machine through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Define initiator, responder, identities, trust assumptions, authentication goals, secrecy goals, compromise model, and terminal states.
Bind ordered message type, role, version, suite, identities, ephemeral values, session ID, sequence, and prior transcript into each state transition.
Use nonces, counters, windows, durable identities, expiry, and idempotent effects according to channel and availability constraints.

modules.title: Encrypted Files, Streams, Chunks, and Format Evolution
modules.description: A backup tool encrypts an unbounded stream under one nonce, writes unauthenticated headers, exposes partial plaintext, and cannot distinguish truncation from a clean end. Learners cumulatively produce seekable authenticated backup container through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Define a bounded versioned file envelope with magic, suite, key reference, KDF parameters, nonce base, chunk policy, metadata, and authenticated lengths.
Derive unique per-chunk nonces and associated data from immutable file identity, epoch, chunk index, length, and final marker.
Authenticate chunk position, total or final state, and file identity to reject deletion, duplication, insertion, reordering, and splicing.

modules.title: Post-Quantum Cryptography and Hybrid Migration
modules.description: A roadmap replaces every algorithm with one new primitive immediately, ignores harvest-now-decrypt-later exposure, and labels a pending module fully compliant. Learners cumulatively produce post-quantum inventory and hybrid migration plan through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Inventory long-lived confidentiality, authentication, signatures, roots, protocols, dependencies, data lifetime, and migration lead time.
Use Go ML-KEM interfaces for key encapsulation with strict key/ciphertext parsing, shared-key custody, failure handling, and hybrid context.
Use Go ML-DSA parameter sets and context-aware signing with explicit key, signature-size, interoperability, and policy tradeoffs.

modules.title: API Misuse, Oracles, Side Channels, and Defensive Boundaries
modules.description: A decryption API reports precise failure stages, accepts attacker-controlled work parameters, retries expensive operations, and exposes timing differences through caches and downstream calls. Learners cumulatively produce cryptographic misuse and oracle threat review through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Identify padding, validity, format, key, user, replay, decompression, and timing oracles across direct and indirect observations.
Bound lengths, counts, nesting, KDF work, signature sets, certificate paths, retries, concurrency, memory, CPU, and deadlines before expensive cryptography.
Keep useful internal typed causes and safe telemetry while exposing uniform bounded failures and terminal cleanup to untrusted callers.

modules.title: Known-Answer Tests, Negative Vectors, Fuzzing, and Interoperability
modules.description: A library has only round-trip tests, generates random fixtures on every run, never mutates tags or headers, and calls 100 percent line coverage cryptographic assurance. Learners cumulatively produce layered cryptographic verification harness through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use authoritative known-answer and protocol vectors with provenance, exact bytes, expected successes, expected failures, and stable fixtures.
Mutate every authenticated field, key, nonce, tag, length, order, version, context, and boundary and require clean rejection without partial effects.
Define properties such as round-trip within valid domains, tamper rejection, parser totality, uniqueness invariants, and version separation without asserting false universal laws.

modules.title: Cryptographic Agility, Dependencies, FIPS, and Policy Evidence
modules.description: A compliance document lists approved algorithms but not the executing module, configuration, boundary, certificate status, dependencies, exceptions, or operating procedures. Learners cumulatively produce algorithm inventory and compliance evidence packet through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Maintain machine-readable use, purpose, owner, suite, key location, dependency, data lifetime, protocol, version, and migration state for cryptographic assets.
Centralize policy and versioned suites, authenticate selection, reject unknowns, measure use, retain read compatibility, and remove obsolete write paths.
Distinguish approved algorithms, Go cryptographic module version, GOFIPS140 selection, enforcement, validation status, operating environment, and application boundary.

modules.title: Production Operations, Incidents, Recovery, and Release Defense
modules.description: A release rotates keys and formats in one irreversible deployment, has no old-data corpus or rollback, monitors only errors, and discovers restore failure during an incident. Learners cumulatively produce cryptographic production defense and recovery drill through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Define low-cardinality success, reject, latency, work-factor, key-version, algorithm, expiry, rotation, migration, dependency, and recovery signals without secret leakage.
Load-test entropy, CPU, memory-hard KDFs, HSM/KMS quotas, certificate services, revocation, key caches, queues, and degraded modes with bounded admission.
Canary reader/writer, algorithm, key, format, certificate, and dependency changes with old/new matrices, dual-read boundaries, telemetry, and rollback.
